Patient.whatItIs: The single Patient resource for this person — name, birth date, sex, languages, addresses, contacts, and links to their primary doctor and clinic.
Patient.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for patient demographics, plus a few facts borrowed from the insurance-claims and provider-directory exports.
Patient.howFaithful: The large majority of fields came through identical or provably equivalent; nearly all the residual gaps exist because this export was privacy-scrubbed or never carried the standardized codes Epic's live server adds.
Patient.whatsIdentical: Birth date, sex/gender, living status, the official name and aliases (including the "call-me" preferred-name marker), race and ethnicity, preferred language, the insurance member ID, and the medical-record identifiers all match exactly.
Patient.whatDiffersAndWhy: Names arrive all-caps ("MANDEL", "JOSHUA") versus Epic's mixed case ("Mandel", "Joshua") — same text, consumers can normalize. The doctor and clinic links use our readable IDs instead of Epic's opaque server IDs, but both point to the same provider and same clinic; the doctor's name also shows more fully here ("Zoe L Rammelkamp") than Epic's masked "Dr. Z Rammelkamp". Epic's live server tags sex and gender identity with extra SNOMED codes; the export kept only the human-readable labels, so we emit those rather than invent codes. The spouse relationship and the contact's employer come through as plain text plus a standard relationship code because the export kept the wording but not Epic's internal relationship code.
Patient.whatsLost: This export was redacted, so phone, email, street address, and contact names arrive as placeholder tokens like "[REDACTED-PHONE-1]" — we pass them through faithfully since that is what the source held. A handful of Epic-only identifiers (portal and internal account IDs) and one prior-address end date are absent from the export entirely and could not be reconstructed; these don't affect identifying the patient.
Patient.mostInterestingTrick: Marital status isn't stored anywhere in the demographics. We recovered "Married" from the patient's insurance-claims data, matching on medical-record number and taking the value that appeared most consistently across the claims.

Condition.whatItIs: This patient's 53 Conditions: 5 ongoing problem-list entries (like "Post concussion syndrome") plus 48 diagnoses attached to specific visits.
Condition.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for the patient's problem list (current and historical) and for the diagnoses recorded at each visit, with diagnosis wording pulled from Epic's diagnosis dictionary and recorded dates cross-checked against the orders and clinical notes from the same visits.
Condition.howFaithful: Almost everything reproduced identically — 1472 fields match exactly, 130 are cosmetically different but equivalent, and only 44 are genuinely unreconstructable.
Condition.whatsIdentical: Every clinical fact a consumer reasons on: active vs. resolved status, the diagnosis text, onset and resolution dates, which problem each visit diagnosis links to, and the total count and problem-vs-visit split.
Condition.whatDiffersAndWhy: The pointers to the patient, the visit, and linked problems use different-looking IDs on each side but resolve to the exact same underlying records, so a consumer following them lands in the same place. Where a standardized code is present on both sides, the human-readable label occasionally differs ("Postconcussion Syndrome" vs. "Postconcussion syndrome (disorder)") while the code itself is identical, so it carries no clinical meaning.
Condition.whatsLost: For most Conditions only the diagnosis wording survives, not a standardized code (SNOMED/ICD), because the export preserves the text but ships no reliable way to map that text back to a code. The visit-type label (such as "Office Visit" or "Telemedicine") shown on 39 visit references is also absent from the export entirely. Both are flagged honestly rather than guessed.
Condition.mostInterestingTrick: Visits record only a midnight date, but a few diagnoses were actually documented a day or two afterward. By checking when each visit's orders were placed and its notes were written, we recovered the true recorded date for those cases — and it matched Epic's own FHIR output exactly on every checkable visit diagnosis.

Encounter.whatItIs: Each Encounter is one of this patient's visits — office visits, telephone contacts, and a couple of facility therapy stays.
Encounter.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables describing patient contacts, their appointments, hospital/facility stays, visit reasons, and the providers and locations involved.
Encounter.howFaithful: Most of the core came through identical or equivalent (subject, status, timing, reasons, participants, account), while visit-type codes and a few coded fields could not be reproduced because the export ships only their text labels.
Encounter.whatsIdentical: The visit identifier, the link to the patient, the "finished" status, the visit start time (matched to the minute including the daylight-saving conversion to UTC), the billing account, the providers and their roles, and the reason-for-visit — recovered as a real coded value, not just text.
Encounter.whatDiffersAndWhy: We set visit class to the standard ambulatory ("AMB") code because FHIR requires the standard value set, whereas Epic's API returns a proprietary in-house class like "HOV" or "Office Visit" — same visit, ours is the conformant form. Display names differ only cosmetically (we keep "Jess Y" / "Assoc Physicians Internal Medicine"; Epic reformats to "YOUNG, JESS" / "MAC APL INTERNAL MEDICINE") and reason text differs only in capitalization ("Transfer Of Care" vs "TRANSFER OF CARE") over an identical underlying code, so consumers see the same person, department, and concept. On lab-only contacts we drop the "provider" participant because the listed provider is a lab machine, not a clinician, matching Epic's API rather than fabricating a person.
Encounter.whatsLost: Visit-type codes and text (e.g. "99396" / "Office Visit" / "Telephone"), admit-source and discharge-disposition codes, the accident-related flag, and most visit end-times are missing because the export carries only descriptive labels or no value for these. Facility therapy visits lose the upper levels of their location hierarchy (e.g. "UnityPoint Health") since the export names only the single department. A few SNOMED reason codes are gone — the diagnosis wording survives as text but the export omits the standardized code. The exact set of visits also differs slightly: Epic's API keeps two empty placeholder contacts and drops about a dozen real telephone visits with notes, and nothing in the export marks which Epic chose to keep.
Encounter.mostInterestingTrick: Rather than guess which visits Epic's API would include, we kept any contact carrying real content (a booked appointment, a facility stay, or a clinical note with a reason) and then automatically pulled back in any visit that an already-built immunization, prescription, or document pointed at — so the included set was justified by real data and no reference ever dangled.

Observation.plainName: Lab results, vital signs & screening findings
Observation.whatItIs: This patient's measurements and findings: vital signs (blood pressure, weight, height), lab results (lipid panel, A1c), screening questionnaires (PHQ-2, AUDIT-C), and tobacco/social history.
Observation.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for flowsheet vital signs, lab orders and results, screening/survey flowsheets, and social history.
Observation.howFaithful: Every actual reading came through identical or provably equivalent, while the residual gaps are standardized codes and entire finding sets that the export never carried.
Observation.whatsIdentical: Every numeric value, its units (weight converted ounces-to-kilograms, height inches-to-centimeters), the time taken, abnormal flags, amended status, the lab result codes, and the lab-to-specimen linkage all reproduced exactly; a "142/74" blood pressure is correctly split into systolic and diastolic components.
Observation.whatDiffersAndWhy: References to patient, visit, and clinician use our own readable IDs instead of Epic's opaque ones, but each was confirmed to point to the same real person, visit, or clinician — a consumer following them lands in the same place. A clinician shows as "Mary S" (a privacy scramble) versus "SMITH, MARY B" — same person. Some timestamps differ by seconds because the export only records times to the minute. A few labels are shorter or longer ("BP" vs "Blood Pressure", "Office Visit" vs the formal visit-type name) but carry the same code underneath, so meaning is unchanged.
Observation.whatsLost: Standardized terminology codes (LOINC for vitals, SNOMED for things like "sitting" or "adult cuff") are not reproduced — Epic assigns these during its own internal build and they aren't in the export, so the export keeps the human wording but not the code. Also absent: 118 "SmartData" findings whose backing data was never shipped, and 75 survey panel/header rows that are just screen-layout groupings with no real measurement behind them.
Observation.mostInterestingTrick: A 2018 lipid panel arrived with its result codes blank. Instead of giving up, the rebuild learned each component's standard code from other orders where the same test was labeled, then filled in the blanks — recovering codes genuinely derivable from the patient's own data rather than copied from the comparison source.

DiagnosticReport.whatItIs: The patient's 9 lab and microbiology reports (lipid panel, basic metabolic panel, and so on), each tying together the order, the visit, and the individual result values.
DiagnosticReport.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for lab orders and their per-analyte results.
DiagnosticReport.howFaithful: Almost everything came through identical or provably equivalent; the only genuine losses are the exact second a report was finalized and the precise identity of the lab organization that ran the test.
DiagnosticReport.whatsIdentical: Report status, the human-readable test name ("Lipid panel"), the category, the specimen-collection time, the order and accession identifiers, and the links to the patient, visit, ordering clinician, and individual result Observations all match exactly.
DiagnosticReport.whatDiffersAndWhy: Epic's own API uses opaque server-generated ids for the patient, visit, clinician, and results while we use readable ids, but each provably points to the same real-world thing, so a consumer following the reference lands in the same place. The ordering clinician shows as "Dr. P Dhillon" versus "DHILLON, PUNEET S" — same person, just a privacy-masked versus fuller spelling. The standardized lab codes (CPT and LOINC) were reconstructed for 7 of 9 reports by matching billing charges and order metadata back to each order, since the export carries no direct link between an order and its codes.
DiagnosticReport.whatsLost: For 6 reports the exact finalize timestamp differs by a few seconds (e.g. 20:41:51 vs 20:42:02) because the export only stores these times rounded to the minute, so the true sub-minute instant can't be recovered. For 8 reports the performing lab organization can't be matched to Epic's own organization record — the export gives the lab's name and a local id but no link to Epic's API id — and the name is sometimes abbreviated server-side (Epic drops "MERITER" from "UPH MADISON MERITER SUNQUEST LAB").
DiagnosticReport.mostInterestingTrick: With no stored link between an order and its billing charge, the standardized procedure code (like CPT 80061 for a lipid panel) was recovered by lining up the charges and the lab orders by service date and finding the one charge that could only belong to that order.

Specimen.whatItIs: Nine Specimen resources for this patient — the physical samples (blood draws, serum, a stool sample) behind each lab result that also appears as a DiagnosticReport.
Specimen.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for lab orders and their results, plus a small, fully-documented lookup table supplying standardized specimen-type codes and human-readable labels.
Specimen.howFaithful: All nine specimens come back with most fields byte-for-byte identical (110 exact), nine fields differ only in how the patient is referenced, and six are an unreproducible empty placeholder code.
Specimen.whatsIdentical: The accession numbers (like H258308 and S141309), the collector's name (e.g. CORNELL, VIRA X), both the collected and received timestamps, the specimen wording ("Blood", "Serum", "Stool"), and the Epic-style specimen-type code all match exactly on all nine.
Specimen.whatDiffersAndWhy: The patient link uses a readable id on our side and Epic's opaque scrambled id on theirs, but both resolve to the same patient, so a consumer following either reference lands in the same place. For the blood specimens we attach the SNOMED "Blood specimen" concept; since the export only ever carries the blood-draw SNOMED, we deliberately leave it off serum and stool samples where copying it would be factually wrong.
Specimen.whatsLost: Epic's six serum specimens carry a hollow SNOMED tag that names the SNOMED system but contains no actual code or label. There is no serum code anywhere in the export to fill it with, so rather than invent one we leave it off — and a consumer loses nothing, because there was never a real code there to read.
Specimen.mostInterestingTrick: The export ships exactly one specimen SNOMED code, and it always means "blood" — even on the parent record of a serum test. Naively copying it would mislabel every serum sample, so the reconstruction only propagates that code when the specimen really is blood, matching Epic's own choice to leave serum samples uncoded.

MedicationRequest.whatItIs: This patient's 18 medication orders (e.g. nortriptyline, loratadine), each holding what was prescribed, the dosing/sig instructions, quantity, refills, and the prescriber.
MedicationRequest.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for medication orders and their dosing and sig details.
MedicationRequest.howFaithful: The core of every order rebuilds identically; the residual is entirely values Epic's server computes or relabels at request time, plus a handful where we ship the export's own honest wording instead of Epic's polished label.
MedicationRequest.whatsIdentical: Order identity, status (active vs. stopped), category, the linked drug, prescribing date, quantity, refills, full sig text, dose, route, frequency, supply duration, and the link from a refill back to its prior prescription all come through exactly.
MedicationRequest.whatDiffersAndWhy: Where the export gives an opaque internal handle and we mint a readable id, the references still point to the same patient, prescriber, encounter, and drug, so following the link lands in the same place. For external "reconciled outside" orders Epic shows a curated provider name while we show the name actually recorded in the export — same source. Drug names differ only in form ("nortriptyline 10 MG capsule" vs. the raw "NORTRIPTYLINE HCL 10 MG PO CAPS"), the same medication spelled differently. A few of our sig texts append the explicit start/stop dates the export records, which is extra detail, not a conflict.
MedicationRequest.whatsLost: Epic's API attaches standardized diagnosis codes to the reason-for-order and a "short course / acute" therapy classification; the export keeps the diagnosis wording but not those server-added codes, so we leave them empty rather than guess. The visit-type label on the linked encounter ("Office Visit", "Telemedicine") also can't be reached from the medication data.
MedicationRequest.mostInterestingTrick: No clock-time is stored for any order, so for a once-daily or nightly drug we read the meaning of the frequency name ("NIGHTLY", "DAILY") and emit a sensible time of day (9 PM or 9 AM), while emitting nothing for as-needed meds that have no scheduled time.

Medication.whatItIs: The drug-identity records for this patient's 18 medications — the "what drug, what form, what strength" half that the prescribing records point to.
Medication.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for medication orders plus a small drug-name lookup table.
Medication.howFaithful: 544 fields came through exactly and none needed loosened matching; the only residual is 60 display-text fields that simply do not exist in the export, so it is highly faithful on everything machine-readable.
Medication.whatsIdentical: Every drug's internal identity, its pharmacy identifier, its dose form code (capsule, tablet, solution, etc.), and the strength for the four single-ingredient drugs (lisinopril 10 mg, sodium chloride 0.9%, and so on) all match exactly, as do the standard drug codes (RxNorm, NDC, ATC, GCN).
Medication.whatDiffersAndWhy: The standard drug codes aren't in this export at all — it ships the drug name but not the coded vocabularies — so they were restored from a separately maintained reference crosswalk keyed off the drug's pharmacy identifier, giving a consumer the same codes either way. The human-readable labels also differ in form: where Epic's API shows the tidy phrase "nortriptyline 10 MG capsule," the export only carries the all-caps order name "NORTRIPTYLINE HCL 10 MG PO CAPS" — same drug, same meaning, just the raw order text instead of a normalized phrase.
Medication.whatsLost: Two purely cosmetic strings: the polished drug/ingredient display phrase (which lives only on Epic's server, so we keep the honest order text instead) and, for 12 medications, the spelled-out form name like "Solution" or "Device" that the export never ships. Capsule and tablet forms do carry the word so those come through, and the coded form is always present — only the friendly label is missing.
Medication.mostInterestingTrick: The dose form was recovered from the very end of each order's description line — "...PO CAPS," "...IV SOLN," "FREESTYLE LIBRE 3 SENSOR MISC" — and that trailing word turns out to be a real form abbreviation that matches Epic's own value exactly, converting what looked like missing data into a perfect match. The spelled-out form name is only filled in when that exact word actually appears in the drug's name, so it's left blank rather than guessed when it doesn't.

Immunization.whatItIs: The 19 vaccine doses this patient received — flu shots, a COVID-19 dose, and others — each with date, lot number, manufacturer, dose amount, body site, and route.
Immunization.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for immunizations, producing one FHIR Immunization per recorded dose.
Immunization.howFaithful: All 19 doses came through with their clinical substance intact; the handful of residuals are FHIR-layer display decorations and a couple of coded units the export never carries.
Immunization.whatsIdentical: Vaccine name, date, status (all "completed"), lot number, expiration date, manufacturer, dose amount and unit (e.g. "0.5 mL"), body site, and route all match exactly. Links to the patient, the visit where a dose was given, and the administering clinician resolve to the same underlying records as Epic's own API.
Immunization.whatDiffersAndWhy: Vaccine names match in wording but differ in capitalization (the export ships "INFLUENZA (FLUARIX) IIV4" upper-case, Epic's API title-cases it), so consumers keying on text should normalize case. The clinician shows as "SMITH, MARY B" versus a privacy-trimmed "Mary S" — same person, different display style. For one dose we name the actual administering department ("MAC APL INTERNAL MEDICINE") where Epic shows only the parent brand ("UnityPoint Health"); ours is more specific and traceable.
Immunization.whatsLost: A few decorations the export omits: the "ages 12+" qualifier on three vaccine names, the visit-type label ("Office Visit") on the linked encounter, and the machine-coded form of the dose unit (the readable "0.5 mL" is present). The standardized CVX vaccine code is also absent; only the NDC code (present on one dose) carries through.
Immunization.mostInterestingTrick: The export records who gave each shot using an internal staff login id, but the clinician directory is keyed by a different provider id. We bridged the two by matching on the clinician's name, attaching the link only when the name matched exactly one provider — otherwise keeping the displayed name and skipping the link rather than risk pointing at the wrong person.

AllergyIntolerance.whatItIs: Four active allergies for this one patient: two drug allergies (sulfa antibiotics and penicillins) and two food/biologic ones (tree nut and peanut), each marked active and high criticality with a "Hives" reaction.
AllergyIntolerance.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables that list each patient's allergies and their reactions, plus a separately reconstructed lookup that maps allergen names back to their standard codes.
AllergyIntolerance.howFaithful: Nearly everything came through identical or provably equivalent — status, type, criticality, allergen, dates, reaction, and even the full standard code sets — with only the allergy category array unreproducible because the source data for it isn't in the export.
AllergyIntolerance.whatsIdentical: Clinical and verification status, allergy type, criticality ("High"), allergen text, onset date, the "Hives" reaction, and the recorded timestamp all match Epic's own FHIR output; for the two drug allergies the full standard coding (SNOMED plus a drug-class code list) was even re-attached and matches Epic exactly.
AllergyIntolerance.whatDiffersAndWhy: Epic stamps a spec-version label on the status code that we omit, but the status meaning is identical so consumers lose nothing; the patient link is written differently but points to the same patient; and the recorded time agrees to the minute, with us declining to invent the extra seconds Epic shows since the export never recorded them.
AllergyIntolerance.whatsLost: The category array (food / medication / biologic) is missing — Epic derives it from an internal allergen catalog that this export doesn't include, so a consumer filtering allergies by category would see these as uncategorized.
AllergyIntolerance.mostInterestingTrick: The export stores allergen names only as plain text with no standard codes, so we rebuilt the codes from a separate name-to-code lookup — restoring the complete drug-class coding for the penicillin and sulfa allergies and a SNOMED code for "Hives," and these matched Epic even though no allergy table in the export contained them.

DocumentReference.whatItIs: For this patient, these are pointers to clinical documents — mostly provider-written visit notes with their full text attached, plus a few imaging reports and patient-summary documents.
DocumentReference.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for clinical notes and the note-to-visit links, plus the exported rich-text note files saved alongside the data.
DocumentReference.howFaithful: Of the three document families, one (the 28 clinician notes) is reconstructed faithfully with all the meaning intact, while the other two — export-time-generated patient summaries and one imaging entry — could not be reproduced at all because the source data simply doesn't contain them.
DocumentReference.whatsIdentical: The clinician notes come through faithfully: the note title text, final-vs-amended status, which visit the note belongs to, the patient and authoring clinician, and the signing/attestation steps. The note body is attached as a file, and the attached bytes were verified to be exactly the original exported note.
DocumentReference.whatDiffersAndWhy: Names and times look different but mean the same thing: the live API masks the author as "Jess Y" while the export carries the fuller "YOUNG, JESS" (same person), and timestamps are rounded to the minute (:48 seconds becomes :00, same moment). We attach notes as plain text where the live API serves HTML — same content, different wrapper. We also include a few extra notes (39 vs 28) because the export never records which notes a site chose to publish, but everything we include is a genuine signed, patient-shared note, so consumers see no fabricated content.
DocumentReference.whatsLost: Two genuine losses. The export keeps note-type and signing-role wording (e.g. "Signer", a progress-note title) but not their standardized codes, so anyone filtering by code rather than text will miss these. And two whole document families — patient-summary (C-CDA) documents and one imaging entry — are generated by Epic at export time and exist nowhere in the underlying data, so they cannot be regenerated.
DocumentReference.mostInterestingTrick: Rather than trust a label, we attach each note's exact original file and fingerprint it, then prove our pointer's fingerprint matches the document's own recorded fingerprint — so if anyone ever swapped in the wrong note's text, the check would fail loudly instead of silently serving the wrong document.

Practitioner.whatItIs: The doctors, nurses, and other clinicians involved in this patient's care — the people that Encounters, orders, and prescriptions reference.
Practitioner.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for the provider directory, supplemented by clinician details mined from the billing/claims tables and looked up in the public federal NPI registry (NPPES).
Practitioner.howFaithful: Most of what Epic's API publishes came through identical or more complete (650 elements exact, 1 equivalent), with about 99 elements that simply can't be reproduced because the source data isn't in the export.
Practitioner.whatsIdentical: Every clinician's name came through perfectly — family, given, and display text — and the overall name structure matches Epic's live API in full.
Practitioner.whatDiffersAndWhy: Two main differences, both still correct. First, names look fuller than Epic's: the live API masks provider names to a title plus initials ("Dr. J Everton") while the export keeps the real name ("Jennifer L Everton") — same person, just more complete, so a consumer loses nothing. Second, NPI, specialty, gender, and the "Dr." prefix were filled in for clinicians who billed on this patient's claims by recovering those values from the claim lines and confirming them against the public NPI registry; these match Epic's values where present.
Practitioner.whatsLost: There's no active/inactive status for any provider — the export simply has no such flag, so anyone filtering on active providers can't rely on it. NPI and specialty are missing for clinicians who never billed on this patient (those values appear nowhere in the export). Some Epic-internal ids for non-physician providers are absent because Epic assigns them server-side and never exports them. And a few of Epic's duplicate/extra listings don't line up one-to-one: Epic lists some providers twice as a feed-merging artifact while the rebuild emits one clean entry each, and one provider known only through a document link couldn't be reconstructed.
Practitioner.mostInterestingTrick: Because the provider directory carries no NPI at all, NPIs were recovered by reading the patient's billing/claim lines — which list a clinician's name and NPI side by side — and matching that name back to the directory, but only when exactly one provider matched and every claim line agreed. That recovered NPI then unlocked a public-registry lookup that backfilled the clinician's specialty, gender, and credentials.

Location.whatItIs: The six physical places tied to this patient's care — five clinics and departments (internal medicine, lab, radiology, two Meriter sites) plus one umbrella place, "UnityPoint Health."
Location.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables listing the patient's clinical departments and one service-area "place."
Location.howFaithful: A clean, perfect result: all six places reproduced and every comparable field an exact match, with nothing that couldn't be reproduced.
Location.whatsIdentical: The display names (e.g., "Associated Physicians Radiology"), the resource type, and the mode ("instance") all match byte-for-byte.
Location.whatDiffersAndWhy: Our resource IDs are short readable values while Epic's are long opaque strings — they look different but point to the same place. Anything referencing a Location (such as an Encounter) is matched by the place's name, which is unique on both sides, so a consumer following the reference lands on the same clinic regardless of which ID style it sees.
Location.whatsLost: Nothing for Location. Some lab-facility extras like alternate names and phone numbers exist, but those belong to the Organization resource, not here.
Location.mostInterestingTrick: Instead of dumping every department in the export — including back-office ones like scheduling and billing the patient never set foot in — the rebuild keeps only the places the patient's actual visits point to. It does this without naming any specific place ahead of time; it reuses the same filter that picks the real visits, so the six places end up being exactly the ones a clinician would recognize.

Organization.whatItIs: The clinics, labs, and insurer connected to this patient: a billing/care facility ("Mac Associated Physicians LLP"), the labs that ran tests, and the payer ("Blue Cross of Wisconsin").
Organization.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for clinic and service-area details, lab directories, lab result metadata, billing-claim images, and insurance-plan records — there is no single organizations file, so each org is assembled from a different part of the export.
Organization.howFaithful: Of the 5 organizations Epic's own FHIR API returns, 4 were rebuilt and 1 cannot be reproduced; nearly everything reproduced matches identically, with only a few cosmetic differences that are provably equivalent.
Organization.whatsIdentical: Organization names, NPIs, tax IDs, provider taxonomy codes, full addresses, the patient and payer each org links to, and active status all come through correctly.
Organization.whatDiffersAndWhy: Names and addresses arrive in ALL CAPS in the export while Epic title-cases them for display ("MAC ASSOCIATED PHYSICIANS LLP" vs "Mac Associated Physicians LLP") — same letters, no meaning lost. A state appears as "WI" in one place and "Wisconsin" in another; both are the same state. One street shows "St" rather than "Street" because the export only ever stored the abbreviation, so a consumer reads the same address either way.
Organization.whatsLost: Phone numbers, the labs' short internal nicknames (like "MHMLAB"), and the "effective since" dates on addresses and IDs are simply not in the export, so they are absent. One historical lab sender (a lab interface retired in 2018) is not in the data at all and cannot be rebuilt.
Organization.mostInterestingTrick: The lab directory in the export has no address fields at all, so each lab's mailing address was recovered from its own test results: the city/state/zip from structured result data, and the street line carved out of the free-text "Testing performed at..." note that accompanies results, cut precisely where the known city name begins.

Coverage.whatItIs: This patient's single insurance coverage — a Blue Cross of Wisconsin PPO plan ("BCBS WI PPO/FEDERAL") on which the patient is their own subscriber, effective 2018-08-09.
Coverage.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for the patient's insurance coverage, the plan and payer reference lists, and one stored insurance claim (the source of the payer's billing address).
Coverage.howFaithful: Almost the entire resource came through identical or provably equivalent — every structural element matched and all three references were confirmed to point at the same entities — leaving only coded labels and cosmetic text that the export never contained.
Coverage.whatsIdentical: Both identifiers (the coverage record number and member number MSJ60249687901), the active status, the group and plan class entries including the plan name "BCBS WI PPO/FEDERAL", the coverage start date, the order, and the payer's billing address (PO BOX 105187, Atlanta GA) all match byte-for-byte.
Coverage.whatDiffersAndWhy: The subscriber, beneficiary, and payer references use different-looking IDs than Epic's API, but each was verified to point at the same patient and same payer, so a consumer resolving them lands in the same place. Epic's API also adds standardized coded labels for payment type, subscriber relationship, and organization type that this export never includes — we keep the plain wording ("Indemnity", "Self") instead, which matters only to a consumer keying off those code systems. Epic title-cases names server-side ("Blue Cross of Wisconsin") while the export stores all-caps, so we preserve the real stored casing; and Epic's "USA" payer country is an Epic-side inference, since the export leaves country blank for US addresses.
Coverage.whatsLost: Only Epic's standardized coded labels for payment type, subscriber relationship, and organization type, plus cosmetic title-casing and an inferred country — none of which exist in this export.
Coverage.mostInterestingTrick: The payer's billing address isn't stored anywhere in the insurance tables, so it was recovered by digging into one of the patient's stored insurance claims, pulling the payer block out of that claim, matching it to this coverage's payer, and rebuilding it as the contained billing organization.

CarePlan.whatItIs: For this patient it holds one big "Plan for Patient Care" roll-up that ties together their active problems, a blood-pressure goal, and the next scheduled visit.
CarePlan.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for the active problem list, the patient's care goal, and the upcoming appointment.
CarePlan.howFaithful: The single longitudinal care plan came through near-complete and equivalent, while three smaller "Patient Instructions" plans could not be reproduced from the structured export.
CarePlan.whatsIdentical: The plan's status and intent, its standard assessment-and-plan category, the four active problems it addresses (GERD, post-concussion syndrome, allergic rhinoconjunctivitis, pollen-food allergy) in the same order, the linked blood-pressure goal, and that it points to a scheduled office visit all match Epic exactly.
CarePlan.whatDiffersAndWhy: Epic stamps the plan with a standardized "longitudinal" code that the export doesn't carry, so we keep the plain-text label — harmless for consumers who match on text. The goal reads "Blood Pressure < 140/90" here versus "below 140/90" in Epic, which only swaps the symbol for the word when rendering; we kept the true source text. The appointment shows as a date only (2027-06-16) instead of a precise time because the export gives the wall-clock time (2:30 PM) but no time-zone offset, and FHIR requires an offset whenever a time is present, so guessing could be wrong; its end time is dropped because no visit-length value ships. Epic's rendered human-readable summary box is a display artifact stored in no column, so it isn't reproduced.
CarePlan.whatsLost: The three short "Patient Instructions" plans aren't generated, because the structured table defining them wasn't included in the export. Their actual instruction text does survive verbatim inside the patient's rich-text note files, so they are recoverable as narrative but not yet built.
CarePlan.mostInterestingTrick: A care team was deliberately NOT invented for this plan: the only available shortcut would have named a former primary-care provider who has left the team while missing two real members, so the project chose to leave it out rather than assert something misleading.

CareTeam.whatItIs: For this patient, this resource is the single care team that groups three providers — Dr. Everton, Dr. Rammelkamp, and Leslie Kommer — as one coordinated team, each with a role such as "Internal Medicine" or "General" PCP.
CareTeam.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for providers and visits.
CareTeam.howFaithful: We produced nothing here, and that is the honest result: this is the one resource we could not reproduce at all, because the export lacks the data that defines the team.
CareTeam.whatsIdentical: The three people on the team are not lost — each already appears in our output as a Practitioner, so the members are fully present; only the grouping into a team is missing.
CareTeam.whatDiffersAndWhy: A CareTeam is essentially a named roster saying "these providers are one team." The export ships the providers individually but never ships the list that ties them together. We could have stitched a team from billing and visit records where the three happen to co-occur, but that would invent a relationship the source never recorded. We also weighed building it from the patient's primary-care-provider history, but that names only Rammelkamp plus a former PCP (Dr. Dhillon, ended Aug 2022) who is deliberately not on the real team, while missing Everton and Kommer — the wrong team, not just a different one. The former-PCP fact instead lives correctly on the Patient resource's general-practitioner field, so a consumer loses nothing actionable.
CareTeam.whatsLost: The team grouping itself and its descriptive codes (the "longitudinal care-coordination" team label and the per-member specialty/role codes). These are assigned by Epic's servers and ship in no included table, so they were unrecoverable even in principle here.
CareTeam.mostInterestingTrick: Rather than guess, the team chased down the patient's actual primary-care-provider history to prove a tempting shortcut was wrong: building from it would have added a doctor who left in 2022 and dropped two doctors who genuinely belong — turning a missing-data problem into a wrong-data problem. Declining to build the resource was the faithful choice.

Goal.whatItIs: A single care goal for this patient: a blood-pressure target ("Blood Pressure < 140/90") set by Dr. Rammelkamp on 2022-08-29.
Goal.rebuiltFrom: Epic export tables for patient goals and their edit history.
Goal.howFaithful: Of the 13 fields, 6 came through exactly and 3 more are equivalent (two references and a provider name); the only true residuals are a missing standardized category code and a one-word wording difference, so every clinical fact survives.
Goal.whatsIdentical: The goal is active, its category reads "Blood Pressure," the start date is 2022-08-29, it points to the right patient by name ("Mandel, Josh C"), and it is attributed to a practitioner.
Goal.whatDiffersAndWhy: Our patient and practitioner references use stable data-derived ids while Epic's API uses opaque ids, but both point to the same patient and the same provider, so resolution is identical. The provider shows fuller as "Zoe L Rammelkamp" where Epic masks it to "Dr. Z Rammelkamp" — same person. The goal text keeps the original "< 140/90" rather than Epic's view-only rewrite to "below 140/90"; a consumer reading either gets the same meaning.
Goal.whatsLost: Epic's API tags the category with a standardized code, but the export ships only the readable label "Blood Pressure," not the code — so anyone matching on coded category values won't have one, though the readable category text is fully present.
Goal.mostInterestingTrick: The "who set this goal" link is stored in the export as an internal login id, not a provider id, so we translated that login id into the provider's actual identity so the reference resolves to the same Dr. Rammelkamp Epic cites — and we pulled the author from the goal's earliest edit but its wording from the latest edit.
